reason: Both correctly diagnose alert noise as the root cause, reject rotation restructuring as the first fix, and prescribe an alert audit with a 'must be actionable and urgent' rule plus weekly review — largely tied on the core recommendation. B pulls ahead by covering key points A omits: it explicitly distinguishes symptom-based (SLO/error-rate/latency) alerts from cause-based (CPU/disk/host) alerts as the actual source of false alarms — a concrete, actionable distinction A never makes — and it explicitly addresses why follow-the-sun isn't viable at 6 people, redirecting that instinct toward fewer pages instead. B also gives quantified expectations (cut 60-80%, <2 pages/shift). Neither nails 'reduced/no sprint commitment during on-call week' explicitly, though B's 'comp time after paged nights' is adjacent. A is clean and correct but is essentially a subset of B's content with no unique facts that would change the asker's next action.
